title: How Transposed Convs Work
concept: Instead of sliding a filter over the input to shrink it, a transposed convolution spreads each input value OUT, multiplying it across a filter-sized region of the output, and sums up wherever those regions overlap.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes a transposed convolution's weights different from simple upsampling's fixed rule?

options.text: They're learned during training via backpropagation
They're randomly reset before every single image
They're copied directly from a regular convolution's filter

exercises.explain: Right — learned weights, adapted through training. Now let's see exactly how those weights turn a small input into a bigger output.

cards.title: Sliding in, spreading out
cards.body: A regular convolution slides a filter OVER the input, shrinking a whole neighborhood of numbers down into one output number at each stop. A transposed convolution runs that motion in reverse: each single input number gets spread OUT into a whole neighborhood of output numbers.

cards.title: Multiply-and-place, not slide-and-shrink
cards.body: For every value in the input, a transposed convolution multiplies it by the entire filter, producing a small filter-sized patch of numbers. That patch gets placed into the (larger) output at a shifting position. Where two placed patches land on the same output spot, their values are simply added together.

cards.title: A tiny worked example
cards.body: Take a 2-number input: [3, 5], and a learned 2-number filter: [1, 2]. Spread the first input value: 3 × [1, 2] = [3, 6], placed at output positions 0 and 1. Spread the second value one step over: 5 × [1, 2] = [5, 10], placed at output positions 1 and 2. Position 1 now has two contributions overlapping, so they add: 6 + 5 = 11. The final output is [3, 11, 10] — three numbers grown from just two.

cards.title: Overlapping rubber stamps
cards.body: Picture each input number as ink loaded onto a rubber stamp shaped like the filter. You press the stamp onto a long strip of paper once per input value, sliding it over a little between presses. Wherever two stamped patches land on the same spot of paper, the ink adds up — that's exactly how overlapping regions sum in a transposed convolution.

cards.title: It's not the input filling in gaps
cards.body: It's easy to picture a transposed convolution as the input numbers just sitting still while empty gaps around them get filled in. That's not it — every single input value is actively multiplied across a whole filter-sized patch and added into the output. Nothing sits still; everything spreads.

cards.title: Sliding-in vs. spreading-out
cards.body: The two operations run in opposite directions:

left.text:
Filter slides OVER input
multiply + sum → ONE output number
per window

right.text:
Each input value spreads OUT
multiply across filter → a patch
overlaps get ADDED together

exercises.prompt: In a transposed convolution, what happens to each single input value?

options.text: It gets multiplied across the entire filter, producing a patch of output numbers
It gets deleted and replaced with zero
It gets averaged with its neighbors before placement

exercises.explain: Right — every input value is multiplied by the whole filter to create a placed patch.

exercises.prompt: True or false: when two spread-out patches land on the same output position in a transposed convolution, only the larger of the two values is kept.
exercises.explain: False — overlapping contributions are added together, not compared and picked.

exercises.sentence: Input value 4 multiplied by filter [1, 3] produces the patch [4, ___].

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes how a transposed convolution builds its output?

options.text: Each input value is multiplied across the whole filter to form a patch, and overlapping patches are summed together in the output
The filter slides over the input and picks the single highest-valued pixel in each window
The output is simply the input values copied side by side with no math involved

exercises.explain: Exactly — multiply-to-patch, then sum-on-overlap is the whole operation.

exercises.prompt: A transposed convolution has a 2-number input [2, 4] and a learned filter [1, 2], spreading with a 1-step shift like in the lesson example. Spreading the first value gives 2 × [1, 2] = [2, 4] at positions 0-1. Spreading the second value gives 4 × [1, 2] = [4, 8] at positions 1-2. What is the final 3-number output after summing the overlap at position 1?
exercises.explain: Right — position 1 receives contributions from both spread patches (4 from the first, 4 from the second), which add to 8. That overlap-and-sum behavior is exactly what causes an artifact you'll meet next: checkerboard patterns.
